title: Workbook — Christian Prayer
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. What is prayer according to the Catechism, and how did Saint Thérèse of Lisieux define it?
  2. Who takes the initiative in prayer: us or God? How does the story of the Samaritan woman illustrate this?
  3. Name the five principal forms of prayer and describe each in one sentence.
  4. What are the three expressions of prayer, and how do they relate to each other?
  5. Name four fundamental attitudes for Christian prayer and identify the parable or Scripture passage that illustrates each.
  6. What are the four main enemies of prayer according to the Catechism? How should we respond to each?

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no «correct» answers to these personal questions.

1. Describe your current prayer routine. What form or expression of prayer comes most naturally to you? What feels difficult or foreign?

Be honest. If you do not have a routine, say so. The important thing is to start from where you are — God meets us in reality, not in ideals.

 

 

 

2. Have you ever experienced a moment when you felt God was truly listening to you or present to you in prayer? What was it like?

If it has never happened, do not worry. Prayer does not depend on feelings. Saint John of the Cross reminds us that dryness can be a deeper form of union.

 

 

 

3. Is there a person or situation you have been praying for persistently (like Saint Monica for Augustine)? Or is there something you have stopped praying for because the answer seemed to take too long?

Monica prayed for seventeen years. Her story teaches us that God's delays are not denials. Write the name or situation here — and recommit to praying for it this week.

 

 

 

4. The Catechism says prayer is a «battle.» What is currently the biggest obstacle in your prayer life? (Distractions, dryness, lack of time, doubts, acedia, or something else?)

Identifying the obstacle honestly is the first step. What one small, concrete change could you make this week to face that obstacle?

 

 

 

5. If you knew with absolute certainty that God is looking at you right now with infinite love, what would you say to him? What would you most want to share?

Write the first thing that comes to mind. Do not overthink it. This is the seed of your personal prayer.

 

 

 

6. Of the five forms of prayer (blessing/adoration, petition, intercession, thanksgiving, praise), make a personal assessment: which do you practice most, and which do you most neglect? Design one small exercise for the form you neglect — something you can try this week.

For example, if you neglect thanksgiving, promise to name three specific things you are grateful for each evening before bed. Write your exercise here.

 

 

 

Practical Activities

A. Pray the Our Father as if for the first time

Take ten minutes. Sit in silence for one minute first, to quiet your heart. Then say the Our Father very slowly, phrase by phrase. Pause after each phrase for a full breath. If a word or phrase catches your attention, stay there longer. When you finish, write which phrase impacted you most and why.

 

 

 

B. Experience all five forms of prayer in one sitting

Set aside fifteen minutes. Spend three minutes on each form of prayer: (1) Adoration — praise God simply for who he is, using Psalm 103 if you need words. (2) Petition — bring your own deepest need to God. (3) Intercession — pray for three specific people. (4) Thanksgiving — name five things you are grateful for today. (5) Praise — close with a psalm of pure praise (try Ps 150). Write which form felt most alive and which felt most difficult.

 

 

 

 

C. Meditate with a psalm using lectio divina

Choose Psalm 63 («My soul thirsts for you»). Read it slowly four times: (1) Lectio — read, and notice what word or phrase stands out. (2) Meditatio — chew on that word; what does it mean? (3) Oratio — speak to God about what you discover. (4) Contemplatio — simply rest in God's presence, without words. Write what you experienced at each step.

 

 

 

 

D. Design your prayer space

Jesus says: «When you pray, go into your room, close the door and pray to your Father, who is unseen» (Mt 6:6). Do you have a place set apart for prayer? If not, create one this week. It can be very simple: a corner with a candle, a crucifix or icon, a comfortable chair. Describe the space you will use. What image or object will help you turn your heart to God?

 

 

 

E. Listen to Taizé music for ten minutes

Search online for «Taizé chant» («Jesus, Remember Me,» «Stay with Me,» or «Ubi Caritas»). Sit in silence, listen, and let the repeated phrases sink into your heart. Do not analyze; simply receive. Afterward, sit in silence for three minutes. Write what you experienced: what did the music do to your soul?

 

 

 

F. Keep a prayer journal for one week

Each day, write briefly: what you said to God, how you felt, what Scripture passage accompanied you, and how the rest of the day went. At the end of the week, read back over everything you wrote. Do you notice any patterns? Any graces you would have missed? Write your summary here.

 

 

 

 

Memory Verse

«Pray without ceasing. Give thanks in all circumstances; for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus for you.»

— 1 Thessalonians 5:17-18

Prayer for This Week

Lord, teach me to pray.
I do not know what to say, I do not know where to begin.
Sometimes I get distracted, sometimes I grow bored,
sometimes I feel as though I am speaking to myself.
But you sought me first.
You thirst for me before I thirst for you.
Take my poor prayer and turn it into a dialogue of love.
Teach me the prayer of adoration, of thanks, of praise.
Teach me the prayer that intercedes for others.
Teach me to persist like Monica, to trust like a child,
to be still long enough to let you look at me.
May my restless heart find rest in you,
and may every surge of my heart rise toward your heart,
until prayer becomes not something I do
but someone I am.
Amen.

Print Workbook
